Name: Experiments
LongName: Reporting Guidelines for Experimental Research: A Report from the Experimental Research Section Standards Committee
Description: A reporting guideline for laboratory, field, and survey experiments in social science, designed to improve transparency and evaluability of experimental design, implementation, attrition, and analysis reporting.
Aim: To ensure experimental manuscripts report enough methodological and results detail for readers and reviewers to evaluate validity, interpret findings, and assess replicability.

Scope.Coverage: Social science manuscripts reporting randomized or quasi-randomized experiments, especially political science lab, field, and survey experiments with treatment and control groups.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of hypotheses, participant recruitment and context, assignment procedures, treatment delivery, participant flow, attrition, and intent-to-treat statistical summaries.
Scope.ContrastWith: This guide is a social-science experiment reporting framework and not a medical trial standard, qualitative reporting guide, or non-experimental observational reporting standard.

primary.description: Gerber AS, Arceneaux K, Boudreau C, et al. Reporting guidelines for experimental research: A report from the Experimental Research Section Standards Committee. Journal of Experimental Political Science. 2014;1(1):81-98. doi:10.1017/xps.2014.11.

items.item_question: Are the specific objectives or hypotheses of the experiment explicitly stated?
items.item_question_background: Explicit hypotheses are needed to evaluate whether analyses and conclusions align with the stated research questions.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the stated research question(s) and specific hypothesis/hypotheses tested.

source_page_refs.quote: A. Hypotheses - State specific objectives or hypotheses.

items.item_question: Are participant eligibility and exclusion criteria reported?
items.item_question_background: Eligibility and exclusion criteria determine who is represented and are required to assess internal and external validity.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract who was eligible, exclusion criteria, and any recruitment-stage changes to eligibility/exclusion.

source_page_refs.quote: Report eligibility and exclusion criteria for participants.

items.item_question: Are procedures used to recruit and select participants described?
items.item_question_background: Recruitment procedures affect sample composition and potential selection bias.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract recruitment and participant selection procedures; if a survey was used, extract survey firm identity and respondent recruitment approach.

source_page_refs.quote: Report procedures used to recruit and select participants.

items.item_question: Are recruitment periods and experiment dates reported, including follow-up measurement dates where relevant?
items.item_question_background: Timing information supports context interpretation, seasonality checks, and reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract dates or time windows for recruitment, experiment conduct, and repeated measurements/follow-up.

source_page_refs.quote: Report recruitment dates defining the periods of recruitment and when the experiments were conducted.

items.item_question: Are settings and locations where data were collected described?
items.item_question_background: Setting and location influence generalizability and practical interpretation of effects.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract data-collection setting(s) and contextual specifics (for example field/lab/classroom, institution type, geographic location).

source_page_refs.quote: Describe settings and locations where the data were collected.

items.item_question: If a survey component exists, are response rate and calculation method reported?
items.item_question_background: Survey response reporting is required to judge nonresponse risk and representativeness.
items.item_extraction_instruction: If applicable, extract survey response rate, denominator definition, and how response rate was calculated.

source_page_refs.quote: If there is a survey: Provide response rate and how it was calculated.

items.item_question: Are details of the assignment-sequence generation procedure reported?
items.item_question_background: Assignment generation detail is needed to evaluate assignment integrity and bias risk.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the procedure used to generate assignment sequence (for example randomization method) and any restrictions such as blocking.

source_page_refs.quote: Report details of the procedure used to generate the assignment sequence.

items.item_question: If random assignment is used, is the unit of randomization reported?
items.item_question_background: Unit-of-randomization clarity is necessary for correct analysis and standard-error estimation.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether random assignment was used and the unit of randomization (for example individual, group, household, cluster).

source_page_refs.quote: Note the unit of randomization (individuals, groups, households, etc.).

items.item_question: If random assignment is used, are baseline means and standard deviations for pretreatment variables reported by group?
items.item_question_background: Baseline covariate reporting helps detect assignment or implementation problems and supports critical appraisal.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether a baseline table is provided by experimental group and whether blocking-related reporting (including weighted summaries where needed) is addressed.

source_page_refs.quote: provide a table ... showing baseline means and standard deviations ... by experimental group

items.item_question: Are interventions in each treatment condition and control condition described in detail?
items.item_question_background: Detailed treatment and control descriptions are required for replication and interpretation of causal contrasts.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract detailed descriptions of each treatment arm and the control condition, including what participants actually received.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide a detailed description of the interventions in each treatment condition, as well as a description of the control group.

items.item_question: Are treatment administration details, anomalies, checks, and fidelity evidence reported?
items.item_question_background: Without administration and fidelity detail, treatment effects may be uninterpretable or non-replicable.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract how and when interventions were administered; delivery mode; software details if computerized; anomalies; manipulation checks; practice rounds; debriefing/interviews/questionnaires; and treatment fidelity evidence.

source_page_refs.quote: State how and when manipulations or interventions were administered.
Report treatment fidelity: Evidence on whether the treatment was delivered as intended.

items.item_question: Are complete treatment materials made available (for example scripts, question wording variants, mailings)?
items.item_question_background: Material availability is central to reproducibility and transparent peer review.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether complete treatment materials are available and where, including scripts, wording variants, and sample materials where relevant.

source_page_refs.quote: verbatim instructions and/or other treatment materials provided in an appendix
Did the author(s) make complete treatment materials available?

items.item_question: Are primary/secondary outcomes and covariates precisely defined?
items.item_question_background: Outcome and covariate definition detail is needed to verify construct validity and analysis reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract definitions of primary and secondary outcomes and covariates, including coding and scale details.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide precise definitions of all primary and secondary measures and covariates.

items.item_question: If indices or survey items are used, are construction details and exact wording reported?
items.item_question_background: Index and wording details are essential to reproduce outcomes and compare effect estimates.
items.item_extraction_instruction: If applicable, extract exact index construction rules and survey question wording (including appendix location).

source_page_refs.quote: For indices, provide exact description of how they are formed. For survey items, provide exact question wording in an appendix.

items.item_question: Are prespecified outcomes/subgroup analyses distinguished from exploratory analyses?
items.item_question_background: Separating prespecified and exploratory analyses reduces selective-reporting ambiguity.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract statements that identify which outcomes/subgroup analyses were prespecified and which were exploratory.

source_page_refs.quote: Clearly state which of the outcomes and subgroup analyses were specified prior to the experiment and which were the result of exploratory analysis.

items.item_question: Is the number of subjects initially assessed for eligibility reported?
items.item_question_background: Starting denominators are required to understand recruitment and potential selection losses.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the number of subjects assessed for eligibility at study entry.

source_page_refs.quote: Number of subjects initially assessed for eligibility for the study.

items.item_question: Are exclusions before random assignment and reasons reported?
items.item_question_background: Pre-assignment exclusions can introduce bias and must be transparent.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract counts and reasons for exclusions prior to random assignment.

source_page_refs.quote: Exclusions prior to random assignment and reasons for the exclusions.

items.item_question: Is the number of subjects initially assigned to each experimental group reported?
items.item_question_background: Group assignment counts are needed for denominator consistency and effect interpretation.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract initial assigned counts for each experimental group.

source_page_refs.quote: Number of subjects initially assigned to each experimental group.

items.item_name: Allocated intervention received and nonreceipt reasons
items.item_question: Is the proportion receiving allocated intervention reported, with reasons for nonreceipt?
items.item_question_background: Nonreceipt/noncompliance can bias estimands if not transparently reported.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract per-group proportion receiving allocated intervention and reasons for nonreceipt.
items.item_expected_location: Results / Participant flow diagram / Compliance reporting

source_page_refs.quote: The proportion of each group that received the allocated intervention and the reasons why subjects did not receive the intended intervention.

items.item_question: Is the number of subjects in each group without outcome data reported?
items.item_question_background: Outcome-missingness by group is critical for attrition-bias assessment.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract counts per group for subjects lacking outcome data and any reasons provided.
items.item_expected_location: Results / Participant flow diagram / Attrition section

source_page_refs.quote: The number of subjects in each group that dropped out or for other reasons do not have outcome data.

items.item_question: Is the number analyzed per group reported with reasons for exclusions?
items.item_question_background: Analysis-population transparency is needed to evaluate analysis validity and attrition handling.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract per-group analyzed counts and reasons for exclusions from analysis.
items.item_expected_location: Results / Participant flow diagram / Statistical analysis

source_page_refs.quote: The number of subjects in each group that are included in the statistical analysis, and the reasons for any exclusions.

items.item_question: Are sample means, standard deviations, and group sizes for outcomes reported using intent-to-treat analysis?
items.item_question_background: ITT descriptive outcome summaries improve transparency and interpretability of group-level outcomes.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract ITT outcome summaries (means, standard deviations, and N per assigned group), including block-weighted summaries if unequal assignment with block randomization.

source_page_refs.quote: Report sample means and standard deviations for the outcome variables using intent-to-treat (ITT) analysis

items.item_question: Is any mismatch between level of analysis and level of randomization addressed with appropriate standard errors?
items.item_question_background: Ignoring assignment-analysis level mismatch can produce incorrect uncertainty estimates.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether level-of-analysis differs from level-of-randomization and how standard errors were adjusted.

source_page_refs.quote: Note whether the level of analysis differs from level of randomization and estimate appropriate standard errors.

items.item_question: Are attrition reasons, missing-data frequencies, and missing-data handling methods reported?
items.item_question_background: Attrition and missing-data choices can materially change estimated effects and must be auditable.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract attrition discussion, missing-data frequencies by group, missing-data methods (for example deletion or imputation), and case-deletion counts/rationales by analysis.

source_page_refs.quote: If there is attrition, discuss reasons for attrition and examine whether attrition is related to pre-treatment variables.

items.item_question: For survey experiments, are weighting procedures described in detail?
items.item_question_background: Weighting choices affect treatment-effect estimates and representativeness.
items.item_extraction_instruction: If applicable, extract survey weighting procedures, variables used, and how weights were applied in analysis.

source_page_refs.quote: For survey experiments: Describe in detail any weighting procedures that are used.

items.item_name: Ethics, registration, funding, conflicts, and replication dataset
items.item_question: Are ethical review, protocol registration, funding role, publication restrictions/conflicts, and replication dataset availability reported?
items.item_question_background: These transparency elements are key for assessing governance, potential bias, and reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract IRB/ethics approval, protocol registration access, funding source and role, publication restrictions/conflicts, and replication dataset URL if available.
items.item_expected_location: Methods / Ethics statement / Funding / Data availability / End matter

source_page_refs.quote: Was the experiment reviewed and approved by an IRB?... If a replication data set is available, provide the URL.

Aliases: Gerber Arceneaux Boudreau Dowling Hillygus Palfrey Biggers Hendry 2014
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a survey experiment in which respondents were randomly assigned to one of three message treatments and a control condition; response rates and assignment procedures are reported in detail.
The manuscript reports unit of randomization, baseline covariate means by treatment group, CONSORT-style participant flow, and ITT outcome summaries.
A laboratory experiment paper describes blocking, manipulation checks, treatment fidelity, and attrition by assigned group, with reasons for exclusions from analysis.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A randomized clinical trial in cardiology that follows CONSORT and does not involve social science lab/field/survey experimental context.
A non-experimental observational social survey without treatment/control assignment.
An engineering materials paper describing stress-testing experiments unrelated to social-science treatment assignment reporting.
Matching.Explain: Use this as a social-science experimental reporting overlay when manuscripts report field/lab/survey randomized experiments with treatment-control design and detailed assignment, participant-flow, and ITT reporting signals.
